title: Plaque Assay for the Aureococcus anophagefferens Virus (AaV)

Archives of Virology, 2002.147(9): p. 1685-1698.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":25,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Make ASP 12 A and autoclave. Do not add solutions after autoclaving. ASP12A Recipe for culturing Aureococcus anophagefferens by Ashley A Humphrey
For bottom agar, add low melting agarose to ASP 12 A for a final concentration of 1%. Sterilize by microwaving. Bring to a boil and let solidify. Repeat three times.
After third microwave sterilization, add vitamin solution and trace metal solutions after allowing to cool for 5 minutes. Pour (15 mL) into each Petri dish.

For top agar, add low melting agarose to ASP 12 A for a final concentration of 0.4%, sterilize by microwaving, then add vitamin and trace metal solutions. Keep at 37º C if performing assay, otherwise let solidify. Follow microwave sterilization process as listed above for bottom agar.
Concentrate 1.0 L, week old Aureococcus by centrifugation (3400 x g for 2 minutes) at 20ºC. 00:02:00
Resuspend to a total volume of (30 mL) in fresh ASP 12 A.
Dilute virus (AaV) in fresh ASP 12 A. We use this approach to titre lysates, but it should work with other samples where a virus is suspected.
Combine (0.9 mL) concentrated Aureococcus with (0.1 mL) diluted virus or control in 15 mL conical tube, invert to mix, and incubate with cap loosened at 19°C for 30 minutes.
Add 3 mL of 37°C top agar to 15 mL conical tube, invert to mix and pour on bottom agar.
Once in plate, gently agitate top agar to ensure even coverage.
Once the plate is solidified, move to a lighted incubator with appropriate light and temperature.
Allow to incubate at 19°C with a 14:10 light dark cycle (100 μE/m -2 s -2 ) for 11 days, face up, to allow plaques to develop. a
creator: Eric Gann
